g.data-narration: Welcome to Graphics Architectures. We'll explore display technologies, frame buffers, GPUs, and the complete graphics pipeline.
g.text: Understanding Display Systems, Frame Buffers & GPUs

g.data-narration: Graphics architectures power everything from simple GUIs to VR. Understanding them is essential for software development and optimization.
g.text: Modern computing relies on graphics for:

g.data-narration: Our learning objectives cover display technologies, raster systems, frame buffers, GPUs, video controllers, input devices, and double buffering.
g.text: 1. Explain display technologies (CRT, LCD, LED, OLED) and their working principles
2. Describe raster scan display systems, resolution, refresh rate, and blanking intervals
3. Analyze frame buffers and video RAM organization with memory calculations
4. Understand GPU functions and the graphics pipeline operations

g.data-narration: Let's explore display technologies: CRT uses electron beams on phosphors, LCD uses liquid crystals with backlights, and OLED has self-emitting pixels.
Raster scan systems sweep the beam row by row from left to right, top to bottom. Refresh rates typically 60Hz or higher.
The frame buffer stores complete images to display. Each pixel location maps to screen pixels. VRAM provides high-bandwidth access.
Let's calculate frame buffer size: 2560×1440 resolution with 32-bit color equals 14.06 MB. Formula: Resolution × Bytes per pixel.
The GPU is a specialized processor for parallel graphics operations. Modern GPUs have thousands of cores running shader programs.
The video controller manages timing and data flow between frame buffer and display. It generates sync signals independently of the CPU.
Graphics systems use specialized input devices: mouse for pointing, tablets for precision drawing, touch screens for direct interaction.
Random scan systems draw lines directly between endpoints, creating smooth lines. They're used in CAD but replaced by raster in mainstream.
Double buffering uses front and back buffers to eliminate flicker. Complete frames are drawn to back buffer, then swapped during V-blank.
For 1920×1080 at 60Hz with 24-bit color, minimum bandwidth is 373.25 MB/s. Modern GPUs provide tens of GB/s.
Remember these key formulas: Frame buffer size equals resolution times bytes per pixel. Bandwidth equals resolution times bytes per pixel times refresh rate.
g.text: Frame Buffer Size = H × V × B

g.data-narration: In summary: We covered display technologies, raster systems, frame buffers, GPUs, video controllers, input devices, and double buffering. Practice these concepts!
